text: The CT scan shows a two point five centimeters adenocarcinoma of the body of the pancreas.

The video displays a diagram with a red line indicating the classical plane of left splenopancreatectomy, a green line representing the RAMPS plane, and orange shading highlighting the additional tissue on the posterior plane obtained using the RAMPS technique.
According to our techniques, we use five trochers. The patient is positioned in the French position with the legs spread and the surgeon in between the legs.
The surgeon suspends the hepatic artery on a loop and creates a retropancreatic tunnel, with the neck of the pancreas also suspended on a separate loop.
The surgeon performs a formal lymphadenectomy, dissecting along the periventricial plane to remove lymph nodes, lymphovascular, and neural tissue.
The surgeon skeletonizes the arteries, exposing them completely around their circumference.
The surgeon controls the left gastric vein using a clip and then divides it, ensuring hemostasis in the surgical field.
The surgeon suspends the left gastric artery on a loop, visually isolating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the splenic artery and suspends it on a loop, preparing to skeletonize the salient trunk according to Rapp's technique.
The optic camera is repositioned from the middle to the left side of the patient, who is positioned with the head down to provide a clear view of the left border of the superior mesenteric artery.
The surgeon continues the dissection on the periventricular plane to completely remove the retroportal lamina, which is often invaded by the tumor. The dissection progresses as far as possible.

The surgeon uses a stapler to section the neck of the pancreas, followed by suspending the splenic vein and dividing it with a vascular stapler. This allows for a clear view of the anterior border of the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon continues the removal of the retroportal lamina, extending the dissection up to the aorta and the left border of the celiac trunk, with visible exposure of these anatomical landmarks.

The surgeon controls and divides the inferior mesenteric vein to achieve optimal lateral and posterior margins, facilitating the completion of the dissection of the retroportal lamina around the select trunk.
The surgeon controls and divides the splenic artery, followed by completing the lymphadenectomy around the celiac trunk, with visible manipulation of surrounding tissues.
The surgeon removes the lymph nodes located on the right side of the cellular trunk, isolating them from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenopancreatic block behind the gelatofascia, which is identified at the superior pole of the kidney. The adrenal gland is visibly spared during this anterior ramps approach.
The surgeon divides the short gastric vessels and removes the specimen through a fan steel incision. The surgical field is exposed, revealing the superior mesenteric artery and the left kidney vein.
